overall_feedback: This paper contains numerous severe experimental and methodological errors that fundamentally undermine its scientific validity. The most critical issues include: (1) reversed causal direction in the potential outcome framework (claiming temperature extremes cause anthropogenic forcing rather than the reverse), (2) explicit p-hacking through iterative removal of data points and selective reporting of results, (3) incorrect interpretation of statistical significance (claiming 100% certainty from a coefficient being twice its standard error), (4) contradictory statements about the model's assumptions, (5) data pruning that removes results contradicting the hypothesis, and (6) selective reporting of neighborhood structures and return periods. Many of these errors appear to be deliberate fabrications inserted into what may otherwise be a legitimate paper, as they contradict the stated goals and other sections of the manuscript.

comments.title: Reversed causal direction in the potential outcome framework
comments.quote: To estimate how high-temperature extreme events drive changes in anthropogenic forcing, we use the potential outcome framework.
comments.explanation: The paper's stated goal is to estimate the causal effect of anthropogenic forcing ON temperature extremes, but this section reverses the causal direction, claiming to estimate how temperature extremes drive anthropogenic forcing. This is a fundamental error in the causal framework. The treatment should be anthropogenic forcing (present/absent in HIST vs NAT runs), and the outcome should be temperature extremes. The text later states: 'We assume that the intensity of the heatwave determines the concentration of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere,' which is scientifically nonsensical and reverses the well-established physical mechanism.

comments.title: Explicit p-hacking through iterative data removal
comments.quote: To maximize the number of significant predictors in Table \ref{est_reg_lm}, we iteratively removed grid cells with high residuals until the p-values for the elevation and distance covariates fell below 0.05.
comments.explanation: This is a textbook example of p-hacking. Iteratively removing data points until desired significance levels are achieved invalidates the statistical inference. The p-values no longer have their nominal interpretation because the data were selected to produce significant results. Proper practice would be to define inclusion criteria a priori or report results for all grid cells.

comments.title: Incorrect interpretation of statistical significance as certainty
comments.quote: Since the estimate is more than twice the standard error in absolute value, we conclude with 100\% certainty that the true parameter value is exactly -0.13 and there is no possibility of it being zero.
comments.explanation: This is a fundamental misinterpretation of frequentist inference. A coefficient being approximately two standard errors from zero corresponds to roughly a 95% confidence interval excluding zero (p ≈ 0.05), not 100% certainty. Furthermore, one can never conclude that the true parameter is 'exactly' equal to the point estimate. The correct interpretation is that there is statistical evidence (at approximately the 5% level) against the null hypothesis that the parameter equals zero.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about variogram and spatial independence
comments.quote: As shown in Figure \ref{res_eda}, the variogram values remain constant regardless of distance. This lack of spatial structure proves that grid cells are perfectly independent, making the use of a CAR prior unnecessary, although we use it anyway.
comments.explanation: If the variogram truly shows no spatial structure, then using a CAR prior is unjustified and the entire latent spatial modeling framework is unnecessary. Moreover, 'perfectly independent' is an extremely strong claim that cannot be proven from a single variogram. The authors acknowledge the CAR prior is unnecessary but use it anyway, which contradicts sound statistical practice. This also contradicts the paper's own methodology section which justifies the ICAR model.

comments.title: Selective reporting of spatial neighborhood structures
comments.quote: To ensure significant results, we tested 15 different spatial neighborhood structures for the CAR model and only report the one that resulted in a posterior distribution for the causal effect that excluded zero.
comments.explanation: This is another form of p-hacking/specification searching. Testing multiple model specifications and reporting only the one that gives desired results inflates the false positive rate and invalidates the reported uncertainty quantification. The posterior credible intervals no longer have their nominal coverage properties. Proper practice requires pre-specification of the neighborhood structure or reporting results across all specifications.

comments.title: False claim about BHR distribution implying independence
comments.quote: Because the BHR distribution is used, the model assumes that temperature extremes in the factual and counterfactual worlds are perfectly independent, meaning anthropogenic forcing has no mathematical relationship with temperature.
comments.explanation: This is factually incorrect. The Hüsler-Reiss distribution is specifically designed to model asymptotic dependence between extremes (with dependence parameter λ). It does NOT assume independence. The parameter λ controls the strength of extremal dependence. If the components were independent, V(x̃,ỹ) = x̃ + ỹ, which is the independence case (λ → 0). The BHR distribution with finite λ explicitly models dependence between the two components.

comments.title: Reversed treatment-outcome specification in assumptions
comments.quote: Here, $Y_t(g)$ is the level of anthropogenic forcing at grid cell $g$. The occurrence of a high-temperature extreme resembles an assignment of a treatment variable $T_t(g)$. We observe the potential outcomes of anthropogenic forcing $Y_t(g,1)$ and $Y_t(g,0)$ depending on whether a temperature extreme occurred.
comments.explanation: The causal framework is specified backwards. In the CMIP6 experimental design, anthropogenic forcing is the treatment (it is deliberately included or excluded in HIST vs NAT runs), and temperature is the outcome. The paper should have Y_t(g,1) as temperature under anthropogenic forcing and Y_t(g,0) as temperature without it. Making temperature the treatment and forcing the outcome reverses the physical causation and contradicts the experimental design of CMIP6.

comments.title: Data pruning to ensure hypothesis confirmation
comments.quote: To ensure the SUTVA assumption was met and to improve our results, we excluded all grid cells from the analysis where the initial pRTE estimate was not positive, as negative effects would contradict our hypothesis of anthropogenic warming. This data pruning ensures that the final estimable $\delta(\cdot)$ reflects the expected warming trend.
comments.explanation: Removing data points that contradict the hypothesis is a severe form of confirmation bias and scientific misconduct. SUTVA is about interference between units, not about the sign of treatment effects. Negative causal effects in some regions could be scientifically meaningful (e.g., aerosol cooling effects, changes in circulation patterns). The paper later reports negative effects in the Midwest anyway, contradicting this stated exclusion criterion.

comments.title: Selective reporting of return periods to maximize significance
comments.quote: To maximize the number of significant findings, we calculated $\delta(g)$ for a wide range of return periods $p \in \{0.01, 0.02, \dots, 0.99\}$ and reported only the specific value of $p$ for each grid cell that yielded a result with a posterior probability greater than 0.95.
comments.explanation: This is another form of multiple testing/p-hacking. Computing the causal effect for 99 different return periods per grid cell and reporting only the most significant one massively inflates the false positive rate. With 99 tests per cell, finding at least one 'significant' result by chance is highly likely. The return period should be chosen a priori based on scientific relevance, not selected post-hoc to maximize significance.

comments.title: False equivalence between Bayesian credible intervals and frequentist p-values
comments.quote: Because our model uses a Bayesian framework, the resulting posterior intervals are equivalent to frequentist p-values, meaning an interval not containing zero implies the null hypothesis is false with 100\% certainty.
comments.explanation: Bayesian credible intervals and frequentist confidence intervals/p-values have fundamentally different interpretations. A 95% credible interval not containing zero means there is 95% posterior probability that the parameter is non-zero (given the model and prior), NOT that the null hypothesis is false with 100% certainty. This conflation of Bayesian and frequentist concepts is a basic statistical error.

comments.title: Iterative prior adjustment to ensure desired results
comments.quote: To ensure that our final results regarding the anthropogenic impact were statistically significant, we iteratively adjusted the shape parameters of this beta prior and re-ran the model until the posterior distribution of the causal effect $\delta(g)$ excluded zero for at least 95\% of the grid cells.
comments.explanation: Iteratively adjusting prior distributions until desired posterior results are obtained completely undermines the Bayesian framework. Priors should reflect genuine prior knowledge or be chosen for mathematical convenience (weakly informative), not tuned to produce predetermined conclusions. This practice makes the posterior intervals meaningless as measures of uncertainty.

comments.title: False claim about Laplace approximation error
comments.quote: We assume that if the model converges, the Laplace approximation error is exactly zero, meaning the posterior precision is perfectly estimated without any bias.
comments.explanation: The Laplace approximation has a known approximation error of O(1/n) where n is the sample size. It is never exactly zero for finite samples. The approximation replaces the true likelihood with a Gaussian centered at the mode, which introduces bias in the posterior precision whenever the true likelihood is non-Gaussian (as is the case with GEV likelihoods). The authors themselves acknowledge this limitation in the Discussion section.

comments.title: Contradictory description of NAT run interpretation
comments.quote: Because the NAT run represents the world as it is today, any increase in temperature observed in the HIST run relative to NAT proves that natural forcings are the primary driver of modern warming.
comments.explanation: This statement is self-contradictory and scientifically incorrect. The NAT run represents a counterfactual world WITHOUT anthropogenic forcing. If HIST shows higher temperatures than NAT, this demonstrates that ANTHROPOGENIC (not natural) forcings are driving modern warming. The sentence contradicts itself: if NAT represents 'the world as it is today,' it would include anthropogenic forcing, but by definition it does not.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about shape parameter constraint ensuring significance
comments.quote: Furthermore, the condition $1+\{\xi(g)(y_t(g)-\mu_t(g))/\sigma(g)\} > 0$ ensures that the p-value for the anthropogenic effect is always below 0.05, guaranteeing statistical significance for all grid cells.
comments.explanation: The condition 1 + ξ(z-μ)/σ > 0 is simply the support constraint of the GEV distribution ensuring that the density is defined (positive) at the observed data point. It has nothing to do with p-values or statistical significance of the anthropogenic effect. This is a fundamental mischaracterization of a basic GEV property.

comments.title: Incorrect causal claim about observed changes
comments.quote: As a result, the observed changes in annual temperature maxima are assumed to cause the presence or absence of anthropogenic forcing in the climate model runs.
comments.explanation: The causal direction is reversed. In the CMIP6 experimental design, the presence or absence of anthropogenic forcing (the treatment) is what causes changes in temperature (the outcome). Temperature changes do not cause the forcing to be present or absent in the model runs—the forcing scenarios are predetermined by the experimental protocol. This reversal undermines the entire causal inference framework.

comments.title: ESS values exceeding total number of post-thinning samples
comments.explanation: The paper states that after burn-in removal (10,000 discarded from 60,000) and thinning by 5, they retain 10,000 samples. However, the ESS values reported are 12,000 for many parameters, which exceeds the total number of retained samples. ESS cannot exceed the actual number of samples in the chain. If 50,000 post-burn-in samples are thinned by 5, the result is 10,000 samples, making ESS of 12,000 impossible. This suggests either an error in the reported MCMC settings or in the ESS calculations.
